caption: Fig.1. Iron corner of diagram for Fe—Mn—Al with $\sim1\%$ C at $1150^{\circ}$ C: 1) alloys with single-phase structure of $\gamma$ -solid solutions; 2) alloys with two-phase structure of $\gamma$ - and $\alpha$ -solid solutions.
Fig. 2. Microstructure of alloys. I) With 25% Mn, 10% Al, 1% C; II) with 20% Mn, 10% Al, 1% C. a) After quenching from 1150°C (× 150); b) after quenching + aging at 600°C for 50 h (× 500).
citation_sentences: In alloys with 1% C, 10% Al, and about 10% Mn the γ-phase decomposes into α-phase and carbides after quenching + aging at 600°C. In the alloy with 15% Mn one observes separate sections of austenite in the microstructure that are retained after aging. With 20% Mn the amount of retained austenite increases and the structure consists of austenite and ferrite with carbides in the austenite grain boundaries. Austenite with an acicular structure is sometimes observed around the ferrite (Fig.2,b, II). With 25% Mn the structure after aging consists of austenite and dispersed phase precipitated in the grain boundaries, which is manifest in the higher etchability of the grain boundaries (Fig.2b). The hardness of the alloy increases sharply after aging — from HRB 86-88 to HRC 35-37.

caption: Fig.3. Mechanical properties of the alloy with 27.5% Mn, 9.1% Al, and 0.92% C in relation to aging temperature (16 h).
citation_sentences: The strength characteristics of the alloys with 30% Mn and 8% Al change little after aging, the plasticity and ductility decreasing somewhat. In alloys with \~9% Al the ultimate strength and yield strength increase with the aging temperature up to 550°C, but the plasticity and ductility decline sharply (Fig.3). Above 550°C the strength begins to decrease and the plasticity to increase due to coalescence of the dispersed phase.
